title: 2D Arrays
description: A grid of values: declaring a 2D array, row/column indexing, and nested loops to visit every cell — essential for grid-based DSA problems.
objectives: Loop through a 2D array with nested for loops
Understand a 2D array as an array of arrays

sections.text: 1. From a Row to a Grid
A regular array is a single row of values. A 2D array is a grid — rows and columns — useful for things like a tic-tac-toe board, a seating chart, or a matrix.
[2][3] means 2 rows and 3 columns — 6 total elements. Each inner {} represents one row.

sections.code:
cout << grid[0][0]; // 1 — row 0, column 0
cout << grid[1][2]; // 6 — row 1, column 2

sections.text: Just like regular arrays, both the row and column indices are zero-based. grid[2][3] itself was only the size declaration — a 2-row, 3-column grid has valid row indices 0-1 and valid column indices 0-2, following the same rule from Arrays.

sections.title: Tracing it
points: Outer loop: row = 0 — inner loop runs col = 0, 1, 2, printing grid[0][0], grid[0][1], grid[0][2] → 1 2 3, then a newline
Outer loop: row = 1 — inner loop runs again, printing grid[1][0], grid[1][1], grid[1][2] → 4 5 6, then a newline
This is the standard pattern for visiting every cell of a 2D array: outer loop for rows, inner loop for columns

sections.text: 6. A 2D Array Is an Array of Arrays

Conceptually, grid is an array of 2 rows, and each row is itself an array of 3 ints. grid[0] refers to the entire first row; grid[0][0] reaches into that row for its first element. This mental model — array of arrays — becomes useful once we reach more advanced STL containers.

sections.title: A movie theater seating chart
sections.text: A theater's seats are naturally described by row and seat number — "Row 3, Seat 7" pinpoints exactly one seat out of the whole grid. seats[3][7] works the same way: two coordinates locate exactly one cell out of the whole grid.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Mixing up row and column order — grid[row][col] is the convention; swapping them accesses the wrong cell.
Using the same loop variable name for both nested loops — like i for both row and column — causing confusion or overwriting the outer loop's counter.
Forgetting that both dimensions are zero-indexed — a 3x4 grid has valid rows 0-2 and valid columns 0-3.
Going out of bounds on either dimension — just like 1D arrays, C++ does not check this, and it's undefined behavior.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: How do you declare a 2D array with 3 rows and 4 columns?
questions.answer: int grid[3][4]; — the first size is the number of rows, the second is the number of columns.

questions.question: How is a specific element accessed in a 2D array?
questions.answer: Using two indices, grid[row][col], both zero-based.

questions.question: What's the typical pattern for visiting every element of a 2D array?
questions.answer: Nested for loops — an outer loop over the rows and an inner loop over the columns, accessing grid[row][col] inside the innermost loop.

questions.question: How can a 2D array be thought of conceptually?
questions.answer: As an array of arrays — grid is an array of rows, and each row is itself an array of individual elements.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A 2D array stores data in a grid of rows and columns, declared like int grid[2][3]; for 2 rows and 3 columns, and accessed with two zero-based indices, grid[row][col]. It can be thought of as an array of arrays — one outer array of rows, where each row is itself an array of elements. Visiting every element typically uses nested for loops: an outer loop over rows and an inner loop over columns, which is the standard pattern for grid-based problems in DSA.

sections.title: Trick to Remember 2D Arrays
sections.text: 2D array = a seating chart. [row][col] pinpoints one seat. Outer loop walks the rows, inner loop walks the seats in each row.
